front: Short Story: narrator reads a history book on 'Benevolence, Righteousness, and Morality' and sees the words 'Eat People' written between the lines
Short Story: ends with the plea 'Save the children...'
Short Story: first major work of modern Chinese literature written in vernacular; published in New Youth magazine
Short Story: frame narrative in which the narrator is said to have recovered and gotten an 'official appointment' to explain his absence
Short Story: narrator remembers a laughing crowd with 'green faces and protruding fangs'
Short Story: narrator believes a neighbor's dog gives him dirty looks because it is in on the cannibal plot
Short Story: narrator's elder brother deals with reports of a famine from Wolf Cub Village
Short Story: narrator believes his brother killed his sister when she was five
Short Story: narrator fears people of his village are trying to get him to commit suicide so they can eat him
Short Story: narrator consults the book 'Medicinal...something or other'; exclaims 'change this minute!' as beams and rafters pile on him
Short Story: after hearing about a nearby famine, the protagonist stops eating meat
Short Story: narrator fixates on a tenant's account of a man who had his organs fried in oil
Novel: protagonist attempts to draw a perfect circle when signing his confession; thinks 'only idiots can make perfect circles'

front: Short Story: a father buys a blood-soaked bread roll (mantou) as a cure for his son's tuberculosis
Short Story: the blood on the bread roll comes from an executed revolutionary, implied to be Qiu Jin
Short Story: title character is a scholar who failed the imperial exams, became impoverished, and resorts to thievery while copying manuscripts
Short Story: title character drags himself by his hands to a tavern to order wine
Short Story: title character dislikes how townspeople call a 'long bench' a 'straight bench'
Author: abandoned a medical career after watching a lantern slide of a Chinese man about to be executed, deciding to treat the spirit of his country's people
Author: wrote in vernacular Chinese (baihua); collections Call to Arms and Wandering; aligned with the May Fourth Movement
Author: preface to Call to Arms describes China as an 'iron house without windows' where sleepers are fated to painlessly suffocate
Author: lectured at a Beijing women's college on 'What Happens After Nora Leaves Home?' and predicted Nora would have nowhere to go
Author: coined the 'gods and demons' (shenmo) genre for Chinese fantasy novels in a 'Brief History' of Chinese fiction
Author: praised by Mao Zedong; the Chinese Communist Party encouraged reading his works during the Cultural Revolution
Author: started a woodcut printing movement popular in 1930s and 1940s China
Author: translated Dead Souls into Chinese
Author: advocated '[worshipping] Darwin and Ibsen'; essays collected in Jottings Under Lamplight
Author: used the example of a man killing his baby to feed his mother to criticize China's overreliance on filial piety
Author: grandfather Zhou Jiefu tried to bribe the chief examiner in the 1890s
Author: died of tuberculosis; photographed by Sha Fei shortly before death
Short Story: a woman tells the story of how her son was eaten by wolves before dying on the day of the title event
Short Story: Si-Ming buys the title scented hygienic object for his wife; a gang makes lewd comments about using it on a young beggar-girl
Short Story: protagonist is obsessed with rereading Romance of the Three Kingdoms; plays on mass queue-cutting during the Xinhai Revolution
Short Story: preceded every production of Gao Xingjian's The Bus Stop in its original run
Short Story: narrator is shocked to learn an old friend is teaching his pupils books of Confucianism
